_doc: Single-stream CPU Q8 parity: fak in-kernel vs llama.cpp CPU, Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct Q8_0, Apple M3 Pro. The canonical SOTA-parity baseline cited by BENCHMARK-AUTHORITY.md. Read these ratios; do not hardcode. This is the HONEST FENCE row (fak trails single-stream raw throughput by design); fak's value is the fleet-reuse layer ON TOP, measured elsewhere. 2026-06-23 refresh: re-measured uncontended on node-macos-a at HEAD 374776a (fak 0.31.0, same llama.cpp build 541bf3762/8200). Reproduces the prior baseline within noise AND adds a thread-count sensitivity arm (-t 6 vs -t 12) that explains a long-standing discrepancy with the stale BENCHMARK-AUTHORITY inline numbers (those cited a non-existent commit 3448d7b and a llama -t 6 decode + an older-build prefill).
machine: node-macos-a / Apple M3 Pro (12 core = 6P+6E, arm64) / 36GB unified / macOS 26.5
regime: single-stream, CPU-only, apples-to-apples (both engines CPU, same machine, same Q8 weights, uncontended box)
methodology: fak arm: cmd/modelbench -hf <safetensors> -lean (quantize-at-load Q8_0), decode 32 steps x10 reps, prefill P=256 x5 reps. llama.cpp arm: llama-bench -ngl 0 (CPU) -p 256 -n 32 -r 10 on the matching q8_0 GGUF. CANONICAL arm pins BOTH engines to the SAME thread budget (12). A SENSITIVITY arm re-runs both at 6 threads (P-cores only) — this is the load-bearing methodology fact below. fak app_version 0.31.0; llama.cpp build 541bf3762 (8200).

metrics._canonical: equal 12-thread budget for both engines (the apples-to-apples thread comparison the prior baseline used; reproduces 2026-06-21 within noise)
metrics._conservative_fence: each engine at its OWN best thread config (fak decode peaks at 12 cores; llama decode peaks at 6 P-cores). This is the most-conservative, least-fak-flattering decode number — quote THIS when a single fence figure is wanted.

prefill_p256_each_at_best.note: both engines' prefill peaks at 12 threads, so prefill each-at-best == same-thread@12

thread_sensitivity.finding: llama.cpp single-stream DECODE is memory-bandwidth-bound and runs FASTER on P-cores only: 68.70 tok/s at -t 6 vs 52.44 at -t 12 (1.31x), and far more stable (stddev 0.19 vs 3.92 tok/s) — the 6 E-cores add contention without bandwidth. fak's pure-Go decode is compute-parallel and wants all 12 cores (38.07 @12 vs 21.81 @6, 1.75x). fak PREFILL is GEMM-bound and scales hard with cores (240.45 @12 vs 35.15 @6, 6.84x). Net: at equal 12-thread budget fak is 0.73x llama decode; at each-engine-best it is 0.55x. The prior BENCHMARK-AUTHORITY inline '0.58x decode (41.9 vs 71.9)' matched a llama -t 6 decode; its '0.45x prefill (247.2 vs 547)' matched an OLDER llama build (the current 541bf3762 build prefills 388-412, not 547).

honesty_fence: fak trails llama.cpp CPU single-stream — decode 0.73x at equal 12-thread budget (0.55x if llama is given its best P-cores-only config), prefill@256 0.58x. llama.cpp has the hand-tuned arm64 NEON GEMM + Accelerate BLAS fak's pure-Go kernel still lacks. This is the documented single-stream loss; fak's win is the cross-agent fleet-reuse layer (4.1x vs tuned warm-KV), not raw tok/s. Never cross-compare the two regimes.
reconciliation_note: The 2026-06-21 artifact reported decode 0.72x / prefill 0.58x at -t 12; BENCHMARK-AUTHORITY.md inline reported 0.58x / 0.45x citing commit 3448d7b (which does not exist in the public repo). This refresh resolves the conflict: both are real but measured different things (thread count + llama build). The canonical same-12-thread ratio is 0.73x/0.58x; the conservative each-at-best decode is 0.55x. See docs/notes/MAC-BENCH-REFRESH-2026-06-23.md.

fak_t12.engine: fak-in-kernel Q8_0 (pure-Go, quantized weights+activations, int8×int8→int32 dot)
